data.title: GrafTech Q1 sales rise 12%, beat estimates on higher volume
data.description:
Overview

  • US graphite electrode maker's Q1 sales rose 12% yr/yr, beating analyst expectations

  • Company posted wider net loss as lower realized prices offset higher sales volume

  • GrafTech reaffirmed full-year volume and cost guidance, pursuing price hikes and trade actions

Outlook

  • GrafTech expects 2026 graphite electrode sales volume to rise 5–10% year-over-year

  • Company anticipates a low single-digit percentage-point decline in 2026 cash cost of goods sold per MT

  • GrafTech is implementing price increases of $600–$1,200 per MT on uncommitted 2026 volume

Result Drivers

  • HIGHER SALES VOLUME - Q1 sales volume rose 14% yr/yr, driving net sales growth, per company

  • LOWER REALIZED PRICES - 5% yr/yr drop in weighted-average realized price offset volume gains, reflecting competitive pressures and overcapacity

  • PRICING AND TRADE ACTIONS - Co implementing price increases on uncommitted volume and supporting trade cases to address weak pricing environment

data.title: U.S. Debt Smashes Through Unthinkable Milestone. Can It Be Stopped? — WSJ
data.description:
By Damian Paletta

Good morning. "The Blob" is a campy 1958 sci fi/horror movie about an alien, erm, blob that lands on Earth and mercilessly consumes everything in its path, growing bigger, meaner and redder as it goes.

The 2026 version would be called "The Debt."

As the Journal's Richard Rubin reports, total U.S. government debt (all $31.265 trillion of it) now eclipses the size of the entire U.S. economy. Don't worry, you don't need to be an economist to understand the math. I can help you. It's bad. Real bad.

Over the past several decades, leaders and lawmakers from both parties have aggressively added to the deficit by spending much more money than they have brought in through taxes. The annual gulf — called the deficit — just adds more and more red ink to the blob, I mean, debt. The deficit in 2026 is projected to be $1.9 trillion.

The government can only sustain this by borrowing more money. And it has to pay interest to borrow money. This has now become astronomical on its own, with interest payments projected to breach $1 trillion this year. Roughly $1 out of every $7 spent by the government this year is going toward interest on the debt.

Neither party has a political incentive to do much about it. It is hard to cut spending (ask Elon Musk) and it is also hard to raise taxes (ask, well, anyone in Washington). So, the debt snowballs.

Reason for hope? Well, a very young Steve McQueen helped (temporarily?) freeze the Blob in 1958. Who wants to star in another sequel?

This is an edition of the Politics newsletter, bringing you an expert guide to what's driving D.C. every day. If you're not subscribed, sign up here.

People and Policies I'm Watching

The price of oil. You really can't look away — prices surged to a wartime high this week on fears that the U.S. might resume military action against Iran to jump-start peace talks, threatening to prolong supply disruptions.

Trump's Friday: The president receives an intelligence briefing at 10 a.m. Eastern time. He goes to the Villages in Florida where he will meet at 3 p.m. with seniors at the Villages Charter School and deliver remarks. At 6:15 p.m., he will attend the Forum Club of the Palm Beaches dinner.

What I'm Following

The White House is set to defy Congress over authorization for the Iran war. The administration is on course to blow past an initial deadline for congressional approval on the grounds that the continuing cease-fire stopped the clock on a 60-day deadline. The notion was met with outrage from Democrats and skepticism from Republicans on Capitol Hill.

Trump pulled the plug on his surgeon-general nomination. MAHA champion Dr. Casey Means couldn't swing enough support in the Senate for confirmation, leading to the withdrawal. She faced pushback during her confirmation hearing over her position on vaccines. Trump said his new nominee would be Dr. Nicole B. Saphier, the director of breast imaging at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center Monmouth and a former Fox News contributor.

The House voted to reopen most of DHS. The bipartisan measure, which passed on a voice vote and ended a 76-day shutdown, funds operations including the Coast Guard, FEMA and TSA — but not DHS immigration-enforcement arms, under a two-track compromise backed by Trump. The second track will allow Republicans to fund ICE and Border Patrol without the votes of Democrats.

What Else Is Happening

  • Sen. John Cornyn, the senior senator from Texas, is locked in the fight of his political life.
  • Maine Gov. Janet Mills dropped out of the Democratic Senate primary, saying she didn't have the financial resources to stay in the race after months of polling behind her progressive opponent Graham Platner.
  • The U.S. will remove tariffs on whisky from the U.K. after Trump's meeting with King Charles III.
  • The U.S. Senate has passed a resolution prohibiting senators from trading on prediction markets.
  • U.S. economic growth picked up in the first quarter.
  • Republican leaders are rushing to redraw congressional maps after the Supreme Court's ruling curbing race in shaping electoral districts.

What I'm Reading

  • Trump Calls Bill Cassidy 'Very Disloyal' Over Surgeon General Nominee Casey Means (New Orleans Times-Picayune)
  • More Than $100 Million Later, GOP Governor's Race Is Neck and Neck (Atlanta Journal-Constitution)
  • An Early Look at Susan Collins vs. Graham Platner, by the Numbers (Portland Press Herald)

This Week in Washington History

On April 30, 1789, George Washington took the first presidential oath of office and delivered the first inaugural address, calling in "humble supplication" on the "benign parent of the human race" to guide the fledgling American government. He made the speech to a joint session of Congress in New York City's Federal Hall.

About Me

I'm Damian Paletta, The Wall Street Journal's Washington coverage chief. I've covered Washington for 22 years as a reporter and editor. I've covered the White House, Congress, national security, the federal budget, economics and multiple market meltdowns.

WSJ Politics brings you an expert guide to what's driving D.C., every weekday morning. Send your feedback to politics@wsj.com (if you're reading this in your inbox, you can just hit reply). This edition was curated and edited in collaboration with Joe Haberstroh and Mali Michelle Fleming. Got a tip for us? Here's how to submit.

data.title: Italy’s next bank deal hangs on time and UniCredit
data.description:
By Neil Unmack

BPM and Monte dei Paschi could combine to create the country’s third big lender. Yet the latter is swallowing a recent takeover, making CEO Luigi Lovaglio unlikely to move fast. But he has an incentive to avoid potential interlopers, like the $115 bln group run by Andrea Orcel.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow @Unmack1 on X

CONTEXT NEWS

Luigi Lovaglio, chief executive of Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena, may sell the bank’s stake in insurer Assicurazioni Generali to fund a takeover of rival Banco BPM, the Financial Times reported on April 24.

Lovaglio in 2025 launched a successful 17 billion euro hostile takeover of rival Mediobanca. In April he led a shareholder campaign to recover the role of chief executive, having been ousted by the board.

data.title: TXNM Energy Q1 GAAP EPS falls
data.description:
Overview

  • US regulated utility's Q1 ongoing EPS rose yr/yr; GAAP EPS declined sharply

  • Company updated 2026-2030 capital investment plan to $10.2 bln

  • Lower GAAP earnings due to higher unrealized investment losses and costs related to pending acquisition

Outlook

  • TXNM Energy does not plan to issue 2026 earnings guidance during pending acquisition

  • Company expects Blackstone Infrastructure acquisition to close in second half of 2026

  • TXNM Energy updates 2026-2030 capital investment plan to $10.2 bln

Result Drivers

  • RATE RELIEF AND HIGHER COSTS - PNM saw rate relief and higher transmission revenues, but these were offset by lower retail load from mild weather and increased expenses from new capital investments and energy storage agreements

  • RATE RECOVERY AND LEGISLATIVE REVENUES - TNMP earnings benefited from rate recovery mechanisms and revenues under Texas House Bill 5247, but were partially offset by lower retail load from mild weather and higher costs from capital investments

  • SHARE ISSUANCE IMPACT - Increased shares outstanding from equity raises in 2025 and 2026 reduced EPS

data.title: Newell Brands beats Q1 revenue estimates, raises FY outlook
data.description:
Overview

  • U.S. consumer goods maker's Q1 sales fell 1.1% but beat analyst expectations

  • Normalized EPS for Q1 was negative but beat analyst expectations

  • Company raised full-year 2026 outlook for sales and normalized EPS

Outlook

  • Newell Brands raises 2026 net sales outlook to flat to 2% growth from (1%) to 1%

  • Company lifts 2026 core sales guidance to (1%) to 1% from (2%) to flat

  • 2026 normalized EPS guidance raised to $0.56-$0.60 from $0.54-$0.60

Result Drivers

  • CONSUMER DEMAND - Higher-than-expected consumer demand, supported by investments in innovation, advertising and promotional activity, drove core sales above company expectations

  • PRICING AND PRODUCTIVITY - Net pricing actions and productivity gains more than offset headwinds from volume decline, inflation and tariffs, supporting gross margin improvement

  • COST MANAGEMENT - Improved operating performance and disciplined cost management contributed to operating margin expansion and normalized EPS above expectations, per CFO Mark Erceg

data.title: Capgemini's AI Approach Doesn't Remove Concerns — Market Talk
data.description: Capgemini's AI narrative fails to mask underlying organic growth concerns, Baader Europe's Maximilien Pascaud writes in a note. The French consulting and technology group delivered higher revenue for the first quarter, mainly driven by AI. "The group is adopting a progressive approach, integrating AI 'by design' into its offerings and evolving toward use cases that are fully embedded in business processes," he says. However, uncertainties persist about margins and the sustainability of contracts in an environment in constant search for value, he adds. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Sterling steady at 10-week high in quiet end to busy week
data.description:
TheMR pound held near a 10-week high against the dollar on Friday as traders digested a week of central bank meetings in which the Bank of England kept interest rates steady and warned of inflation risks stemming from the Iran war.

Sterling was little changed at $1.3606 and a touch softer against the euro at 86.32 pence, after jumping sharply against both on Thursday.

ING analysts said the previous session's unusual rally "may have been a function of month-end flows, where equity portfolio managers were rebalancing into UK asset markets after their underperformance in April."

Fund managers typically target fixed allocations across regions and periodically buy or sell assets to restore those weightings after market moves.

Liquidity on Friday was thin due to holidays across much of Europe, while the bigger medium-term focus for the pound was Thursday's BoE meeting.

The bank outlined a wide range of possible economic impacts from the Iran war, from scenarios that might require "forceful" rate rises to others that may warrant no increase at all.

Governor Andrew Bailey said policymakers faced a "difficult judgement call" over coming months, warning that waiting for clear evidence of inflationary pressures could leave the BoE acting too late.

He said he did not want to push back against market expectations for at least two rate rises this year and described policy as being on an "active hold".

Markets see a June rate hike as roughly a coin toss and price in two 25-basis-point increases across the BoE's three meetings through September.

But analysts say uncertainty is high.

"A world where the supply of commodities out of the Middle East starts to normalise in the coming weeks would mean very little chance of policy tightening this year, we think - and no chance of a June hike," said Morgan Stanley analysts.

"In a scenario where the current level of disruptions in the supply of oil and gas persist for months, and commodity prices readjust higher, hikes become likely."

The European Central Bank, Federal Reserve and Bank of Japan also all left rates steady this week.
